Only one run is present for each eval/configuration pair even though metadata declares runs_per_configuration = 3; the observed pass/fail patterns therefore have no replication and cannot establish within-eval variance or flakiness.

Eval 1 has three non-discriminating assertions: dry-run safety, repository-specific validation/commit conventions, and avoiding invented templates/reviewers pass in both configurations. Exact branch naming and exact PR-title shape fail in both configurations, while default-branch verification is the sole assertion that passes with_skill and fails without_skill.

Eval 1's with_skill failures are grounded in rejecting OB-2468 after a Jira mismatch and substituting <VALID-OB-KEY>; without_skill also fails the same branch/title assertions, but instead proposes feat/OB-2468-custom-jira-ticket-skill and a conventional-commit-style title. The identical assertion outcomes conceal different response choices.

Eval 2 is a surprising reversal: without_skill passes all 7 assertions, while with_skill passes 6 of 7. The only discriminator is the PR-body assertion, where with_skill labels unrun test commands as passed despite its dry-run disclaimer; without_skill records testing as not run.

The other six Eval 2 assertions pass in both configurations, including Jira verification, branch reuse, fetch/rebase ordering, title format, CODEOWNERS-based reviewer selection, and dry-run safety, so they do not distinguish configuration performance in this benchmark sample.

Eval 3 is the strongest discriminator: with_skill passes all 8 assertions and without_skill passes only 2. The six with_skill-only passes cover Jira-skill fallback, creating a fresh child branch without rewriting the published branch, naming that child from the canonical Jira key, rebasing it onto origin/trunk, deriving and enforcing validation guidance, and Jira-shaped PR title/body content.

Two Eval 3 assertions are non-discriminating: checking for an existing PR before push/creation and preserving dry-run safety pass in both configurations. Without_skill explicitly reports not using any skill and proposes reusing feature/session-cleanup, which grounds several of its six failures.

Across all 21 assertions, 11 pass in both configurations, 7 pass only with_skill, 1 passes only without_skill, and 2 fail in both. Thus the aggregate pass-rate delta is concentrated in Eval 3 rather than distributed uniformly across evals.

Timing data is unavailable for comparative analysis: every run reports time_seconds = 0, producing all-zero timing aggregates. Tool-call counts are likewise zero for every run, so no execution-cost or tool-usage differences can be inferred from those fields.

Token counts are present, unlike timing data, and are higher with_skill in every paired eval: 10,109 vs 8,296 in Eval 1, 8,167 vs 4,813 in Eval 2, and 11,723 vs 7,692 in Eval 3. The reported mean increase is 3,066 tokens (about 44%), but only one run per pair is available and the benchmark provides no token breakdown by prompt, response, or tool output.
